FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes ABCD Study Year 3 (2019-2021) data, ABCD 5.1 release, 21 U.S. sites, N=10,092 early adolescents aged 11-15.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data using Chi-square tests and independent samples t-tests to describe social media use patterns.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Academic Pediatrics (2025); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Phone/SM-specific variables are present (time on named platforms TikTok/Instagram/Snapchat/YouTube, total social media time h/d, SMAQ score, number of accounts, secret account status), but every analysis pairs these SM variables against demographic-only variables (sex: male vs female; age group: under 13 vs 13+). No individual-trait-level variable (health, behavioral, cognitive, well-being, neural, etc.) appears on the other side of any analysis. SMAQ is itself an SM/phone variable, not an individual-trait outcome. The entire paper is descriptive prevalence/pattern analysis stratified by demographics only. FT-IC2 NOT_MET: other-side is demographic-only.

notes: Descriptive epidemiology paper reporting prevalence and patterns of SM use by age and sex only. No individual-trait outcome analyzed in relation to SM.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes ABCD Study Year 2 and Year 3 follow-up data, recruited from 21 U.S. sites, N=8006 early adolescents aged 11-12 at Year 2.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective logistic regression analysis of ABCD data examining screen modalities and substance use experimentation.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Drug and Alcohol Dependence (2025); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: social media time (h/d) has its own AOR estimate paired with substance use experimentation (individual-trait outcome). E.g., AOR for social media -> any substance experimentation = 1.20 (95% CI 1.14-1.26); texting -> any substance = AOR 1.18 (95% CI 1.12-1.24); video chatting -> any substance = AOR 1.09 (95% CI 1.03-1.16). Social media and texting are phone/SM-specific variables; substance use (alcohol, nicotine, cannabis experimentation) is an individual-trait-level outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple logistic regression models with AORs and 95% CIs reported for each screen modality paired with each substance use outcome (Fig. 1 and text). Inferential analysis present.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric AOR estimates with 95% CIs reported throughout (e.g., social media AOR 1.20, 95% CI 1.14-1.26 for any substance; texting AOR 1.18, 95% CI 1.12-1.24).

FT_EC7_reason: Social media/texting/video chat are IVs predicting substance use as DV in the prospective logistic regression analyses. SM is not DV-only; condition (b) of FT-EC7 is not met.
notes: Clear qualifying paper: phone/SM-specific variables (social media, texting, video chat) as IVs predicting substance use outcomes (individual-trait). Prospective cohort design.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes ABCD Study longitudinal data from Years 2 (2018-2020), 3 (2019-2021), and 4 (2020-2022), 21 U.S. sites, N=7691 early adolescents.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical mediation analysis (generalized structural equation modeling, GSEM) of ABCD data examining social media time, cannabis expectancies, and cannabis experimentation.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Drug and Alcohol Dependence (2025); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: social media time (h/d, Year 2) as IV with its own B coefficient paired with cannabis experimentation (Year 4) as DV: B=0.18 (95% CI 0.13-0.24, p<0.001). Social media time is a phone/SM-specific variable; cannabis experimentation is an individual-trait-level substance use outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: GSEM produces direct path coefficient B=0.18 (95% CI 0.13-0.24) for social media->cannabis experimentation; indirect effect via positive expectancies B=0.05 (95% CI 0.03-0.06); indirect effect via negative expectancies B=0.03 (95% CI 0.02-0.04). Inferential analysis with quantitative results present.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric path coefficients (B), 95% CIs, and mediation percentages (19.8% positive expectancies, 13.6% negative expectancies) reported for the SM-cannabis relationship.

FT_EC7_reason: Social media time is the IV predicting cannabis experimentation as DV. SM is not DV-only; condition (b) of FT-EC7 is not met.
notes: Prospective mediation study: social media (IV) -> cannabis expectancies (mediator) -> cannabis experimentation (DV). Clearly qualifies. Cannabis expectancies as mediator do not affect IC2 since SM is IV with its own direct coefficient.
